title: Subarray sum equals k
description: Count subarrays whose sum equals k

examples.explanation: Start with count = 0. i=0: current_sum=0. j=0: add arr[0]=1, current_sum=1, not equal to 2. j=1: add arr[1]=1, current_sum=2, equal to 2, count=1. j=2: add arr[2]=1, current_sum=3, not equal to 2. i=1: current_sum=0. j=1: add arr[1]=1, current_sum=1, not equal to 2. j=2: add arr[2]=1, current_sum=2, equal to 2, count=2. i=2: current_sum=0. j=2: add arr[2]=1, current_sum=1, not equal to 2. End loops, return count=2.

Start with count = 0. i=0: current_sum=0. j=0: add arr[0]=1, current_sum=1, not equal to 3. j=1: add arr[1]=2, current_sum=3, equal to 3, count=1. j=2: add arr[2]=3, current_sum=6, not equal to 3. i=1: current_sum=0. j=1: add arr[1]=2, current_sum=2, not equal to 3. j=2: add arr[2]=3, current_sum=5, not equal to 3. i=2: current_sum=0. j=2: add arr[2]=3, current_sum=3, equal to 3, count=2. End loops, return count=2.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Check all possible subarrays by iterating through each starting index and calculating sums for all ending indices. This straightforward method guarantees correctness but becomes inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Subarrays with Sum Equals k Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the O(n²) brute force approach to count all subarrays whose sum equals k.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the problem by systematically checking every possible subarray in the array. The intuition is straightforward: since we need to count subarrays with a specific sum, we can examine all contiguous segments of the array. This guarantees correctness because no subarray is missed. We iterate through each starting index i from 0 to n-1. For each i, we initialize a running sum to 0 and iterate through each ending index j from i to n-1. At each j, we add arr[j] to the running sum and check if it equals k. If it does, we increment our count. This method is exhaustive but inefficient for large n due to its quadratic time complexity. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize count = 0. 2. For i from 0 to n-1: a. Initialize current_sum = 0. b. For j from i to n-1: i. Add arr[j] to current_sum. ii. If current_sum == k, increment count. 3. Return count. Time complexity is O(n²) because of the nested loops over n elements. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the full nested loop structure. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds or forgetting to reset current_sum for each starting index. Real code pattern: def count_subarrays(arr, k): count = 0; n = len(arr); for i in range(n): current_sum = 0; for j in range(i, n): current_sum += arr[j]; if current_sum == k: count += 1; return count.

examples.trace: Start with count = 0. i=0: current_sum=0. j=0: add arr[0]=1, current_sum=1, not equal to 2. j=1: add arr[1]=1, current_sum=2, equal to 2, count=1. j=2: add arr[2]=1, current_sum=3, not equal to 2. i=1: current_sum=0. j=1: add arr[1]=1, current_sum=1, not equal to 2. j=2: add arr[2]=1, current_sum=2, equal to 2, count=2. i=2: current_sum=0. j=2: add arr[2]=1, current_sum=1, not equal to 2. End loops, return count=2.

Start with count = 0. i=0: current_sum=0. j=0: add arr[0]=1, current_sum=1, not equal to 3. j=1: add arr[1]=2, current_sum=3, equal to 3, count=1. j=2: add arr[2]=3, current_sum=6, not equal to 3. i=1: current_sum=0. j=1: add arr[1]=2, current_sum=2, not equal to 3. j=2: add arr[2]=3, current_sum=5, not equal to 3. i=2: current_sum=0. j=2: add arr[2]=3, current_sum=3, equal to 3, count=2. End loops, return count=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Nested Loop to Generate All Subarrays
ladders.desc: Implement the nested loop structure that iterates through all starting and ending indices to generate every possible subarray.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the core of the brute force approach by generating all contiguous subarrays of an array. The intuition is that to examine every subarray, we need to consider all possible starting points and, for each starting point, all possible ending points that come after it. This ensures we cover every segment without repetition or omission. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. For i from 0 to n-1 (starting index): a. For j from i to n-1 (ending index): i. The subarray is from index i to j inclusive. ii. Perform an operation on this subarray (e.g., calculate sum). This generates n*(n+1)/2 subarrays in total. Time complexity is O(n²) due to the nested loops. Space complexity is O(1) if we only track indices. This connects to L0 by providing the framework to which we add sum calculation and comparison. In L0, we insert sum accumulation and k-check inside the inner loop. Common mistakes include using j < i or incorrect loop ranges, which might skip subarrays or cause index errors. Real code pattern: for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): subarray = arr[i:j+1] # or process directly without slicing.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Accumulate Sum and Compare with Target k
ladders.desc: Compute the sum of a subarray dynamically and check if it matches the target value k.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves maintaining a running sum as we extend a subarray and comparing it to k at each step. The intuition is that instead of recalculating the sum from scratch for each subarray, we can build it incrementally, which is more efficient within the nested loop structure. For a fixed starting index i, we initialize current_sum to 0. Then, as we iterate j from i to n-1, we add arr[j] to current_sum. After each addition, we check if current_sum equals k. If yes, we count it. This method avoids redundant calculations but still operates in O(n²) time due to the outer loop. Step-by-step: 1. Start with current_sum = 0. 2. For each element in the subarray (from i to j): a. Add element to current_sum. b. If current_sum == k, record a match. Time complexity for this logic per starting index is O(n), leading to O(n²) overall. Space is O(1). This connects to L1 by adding the sum accumulation and comparison inside the inner loop. Common mistakes include resetting current_sum incorrectly or comparing before adding. Real code pattern: current_sum = 0; for j in range(i, n): current_sum += arr[j]; if current_sum == k: count += 1.

examples.trace: Start current_sum=0. j=0: add arr[0]=2, current_sum=2, not equal to 5. j=1: add arr[1]=3, current_sum=5, equal to 5, match found. j=2: add arr[2]=4, current_sum=9, not equal to 5.
Start current_sum=0. j=0: add arr[0]=1, current_sum=1, not equal to 0. j=1: add arr[1]=-1, current_sum=0, equal to 0, match found. j=2: add arr[2]=1, current_sum=1, not equal to 0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Through Array with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Use nested loops to access all pairs of indices (i, j) where i ≤ j, representing subarray boundaries.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the loop mechanics to traverse all valid starting and ending index pairs. The intuition is that a subarray is defined by two indices: start i and end j, with i ≤ j. By using an outer loop for i and an inner loop for j starting from i, we ensure we cover every possible contiguous segment without gaps or overlaps. This is a fundamental pattern in array processing. Step-by-step: 1. Outer loop: i from 0 to n-1. 2. Inner loop: j from i to n-1. 3. For each pair (i, j), the subarray is arr[i..j]. Time complexity is O(n²) as the inner loop runs n-i times for each i, summing to about n²/2 iterations. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 by providing the index pairs over which we accumulate sums. In L2, we add the sum calculation within the inner loop. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors, such as using j < n instead of j ≤ n-1, or starting j from 0. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): for j in range(i, len(arr)): # process arr[i:j+1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access Array Elements and Update a Running Sum
ladders.desc: Retrieve elements from an array sequentially and maintain a cumulative total by addition.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two key actions: accessing array elements by index and updating a running sum through addition. The intuition is that to compute the sum of a subarray, we need to fetch each element in order and add it to an accumulator. This is fundamental in many array algorithms. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize a variable (e.g., current_sum) to 0. 2. Loop through indices from a start to an end point. 3. For each index, access the array element using arr[index]. 4. Add the element's value to current_sum. 5. Optionally, check or use the sum after each addition. Time complexity is O(m) for m elements accessed. Space complexity is O(1) as we use a constant amount of extra memory. This connects to L3 by being the operation performed for each index pair (i, j) in the nested loops. In L3, we iterate over indices, and here we define what to do with each index: access and add. Common mistakes include using the wrong index, not initializing the sum to 0, or adding in the wrong order. Real code pattern: current_sum = 0; for idx in range(start, end+1): current_sum += arr[idx].

examples.trace: Initialize current_sum=0. idx=0: access arr[0]=5, current_sum=0+5=5. idx=1: access arr[1]=3, current_sum=5+3=8. idx=2: access arr[2]=2, current_sum=8+2=10. Final sum=10.
Initialize current_sum=0. idx=1: access arr[1]=-1, current_sum=0+(-1)=-1. idx=2: access arr[2]=1, current_sum=-1+1=0. Final sum=0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Arrays and Basic Loops
ladders.desc: Learn how arrays store elements and use simple loops to traverse them.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the basics of arrays and loop iteration, which are prerequisites for the brute force approach. An array is a data structure that holds a collection of elements, each accessible by an index starting from 0. The intuition is that to process an array, we often need to visit each element in sequence, which is done using loops. A for loop allows us to repeat an action for a specified range of indices. Step-by-step: 1. Define an array, e.g., arr = [a0, a1, ..., an-1] with n elements. 2. Use a loop to iterate from index 0 to n-1. 3. In each iteration, access the element via arr[index]. 4. Perform an operation, such as printing or adding to a sum. Time complexity for traversing an array once is O(n). Space complexity is O(1) if no extra data structures are used. This connects to L4 by providing the mechanism to access array elements; in L4, we extend this to multiple loops and sum updates. Common mistakes include indexing errors (e.g., using index n which is out of bounds) or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: arr = [1, 2, 3]; for i in range(len(arr)): print(arr[i]).

examples.trace: Array has 3 elements: index 0=10, index 1=20, index 2=30. Loop i from 0 to 2: i=0 access arr[0]=10, i=1 access arr[1]=20, i=2 access arr[2]=30.
Array has 2 elements: index 0=5, index 1=15. Loop i from 0 to 1: i=0 access arr[0]=5, i=1 access arr[1]=15.

approaches.name: Prefix Sum with HashMap O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a hashmap to store cumulative prefix sums and their frequencies while iterating. For each element, check if (current_sum - k) exists in the map to count valid subarrays ending at that position.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Subarrays with Sum Equals K Using Prefix Sum and HashMap
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting all subarrays whose sum equals k using prefix sums and hashmap frequency tracking.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting all contiguous subarrays whose elements sum to exactly k. The naive approach would check all O(n²) subarrays, but we can achieve O(n) using prefix sums and a hashmap. The key insight is that for any subarray from index i to j, its sum equals prefix_sum[j] - prefix_sum[i-1]. If we want this to equal k, then prefix_sum[j] - k = prefix_sum[i-1]. So as we iterate through the array, we maintain a running cumulative sum. For each position j, we check if (current_sum - k) exists in our hashmap of previously seen prefix sums. Each occurrence represents a starting index i-1 where the subarray from i to j sums to k. We also need to handle the case where the subarray starts at index 0, which is why we initialize the hashmap with {0:1}. The algorithm: 1. Initialize count=0, current_sum=0, prefix_map={0:1}. 2. Iterate through each num in array. 3. Add num to current_sum. 4. If (current_sum - k) exists in prefix_map, add its frequency to count. 5. Increment frequency of current_sum in prefix_map. 6. Return count. Time complexity is O(n) since we make constant-time operations for each element. Space complexity is O(n) for the hashmap in worst case when all prefix sums are distinct. This approach transforms the problem from checking all subarrays to checking for complement sums in a hashmap, dramatically improving efficiency. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize prefix_map with {0:1} (missing subarrays starting at index 0), not updating the map before checking (which would count subarrays of length 0), or misunderstanding that we're counting frequencies of prefix sums, not just their existence. The connection to L1 is that this level implements the complete algorithm using the prefix sum frequency tracking subroutine.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Track Prefix Sum Frequencies with HashMap
ladders.desc: Maintain a hashmap that stores cumulative sums and their occurrence counts while iterating through an array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the engine of the full solution. We need to efficiently answer: 'How many times have we seen a particular cumulative sum so far?' A hashmap provides O(1) lookups and updates. The algorithm: Initialize an empty hashmap (dictionary). For each element in the array: 1. Update the running sum. 2. Check if a target value exists in the map (this will be used in L0 to find current_sum - k). 3. Update the frequency of the current sum in the map. The critical detail is that we must initialize the map with {0:1} to handle subarrays starting at index 0. Why? When the running sum itself equals k, we need to count that subarray from index 0 to current position. Mathematically, current_sum - k = 0, so we need to know that sum 0 has occurred once (before we started). Time complexity is O(n) for n elements, with O(1) operations per element. Space complexity is O(n) in worst case when all prefix sums are distinct. This feeds into L0 by providing the mechanism to quickly find how many starting indices yield a valid subarray ending at current position. Common mistakes include updating the map after checking (should be before or after depending on whether we want to include current element in the check—in our case, we check first, then update), or not handling the initial 0 sum properly. The intuition is that by storing frequencies, we can count multiple subarrays with the same starting sum efficiently.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Transform Subarray Sum Problem into Prefix Sum Difference Problem
ladders.desc: Convert the problem of finding subarrays with sum k into finding pairs of prefix sums whose difference equals k.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is mathematical: For any subarray from index i to j (inclusive), its sum S = nums[i] + nums[i+1] + ... + nums[j]. Let prefix_sum[j] be the sum of elements from index 0 to j. Then prefix_sum[j] = nums[0] + ... + nums[j]. Similarly, prefix_sum[i-1] = nums[0] + ... + nums[i-1] (or 0 if i=0). Notice that S = prefix_sum[j] - prefix_sum[i-1]. We want S = k, so prefix_sum[j] - prefix_sum[i-1] = k, which rearranges to prefix_sum[j] - k = prefix_sum[i-1]. This transforms the problem from 'find all (i,j) with subarray sum k' to 'for each j, count how many i-1 have prefix_sum[i-1] = prefix_sum[j] - k'. Since i-1 < j, we only need to look at prefix sums before position j. This is why in L1 we maintain a hashmap of previous prefix sums. The algorithm for this level: Given an array, compute all prefix sums. Then for each prefix_sum[j], count how many earlier prefix_sum[i-1] equal prefix_sum[j] - k. Sum these counts for all j. Time complexity O(n²) if done naively by checking all pairs, but sets up the optimization in L1. Space complexity O(n) to store prefix sums. This feeds into L1 by showing why we need to track frequencies of prefix sums. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices, or misunderstanding that i can be 0 (then prefix_sum[i-1] is defined as 0). The intuition is that instead of thinking about subarrays directly, we think about differences between cumulative sums.

examples.input: nums = [1,1,1], k = 2, find prefix sums and count differences
examples.trace:
Prefix sums: [1,2,3]
For j=0: prefix_sum[0]=1, need prefix_sum[i-1]=1-2=-1, no earlier sums, count=0
For j=1: prefix_sum[1]=2, need prefix_sum[i-1]=2-2=0, i-1=-1 means i=0, count 1 (by definition), count=1
For j=2: prefix_sum[2]=3, need prefix_sum[i-1]=3-2=1, earlier sums at i-1=0 has value 1, count=1, total count=2

examples.input: nums = [1,2,3], k = 3, find prefix sums and count differences
examples.trace:
Prefix sums: [1,3,6]
For j=0: prefix_sum[0]=1, need 1-3=-2, no, count=0
For j=1: prefix_sum[1]=3, need 3-3=0, i=0 case, count=1
For j=2: prefix_sum[2]=6, need 6-3=3, earlier sum at i-1=1 has value 3, count=1, total=2

testCases.input: nums = [1,1,1], k = 2, return count using prefix sum difference logic
nums = [1,2,3], k = 3, return count using prefix sum difference logic
nums = [1,-1,0], k = 0, return count using prefix sum difference logic
nums = [2,2], k = 4, return count using prefix sum difference logic
nums = [1,2,3,4], k = 6, return count using prefix sum difference logic
nums = [0,0], k = 0, return count using prefix sum difference logic
nums = [5], k = 5, return count using prefix sum difference logic
nums = [1,1], k = 1, return count using prefix sum difference logic
nums = [3,1,2], k = 3, return count using prefix sum difference logic
nums = [1,2,1,2], k = 3, return count using prefix sum difference logic

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Prefix Sums of an Array
ladders.desc: Calculate cumulative sums where each element is the sum of all previous elements up to that index.
ladders.explanation: A prefix sum array transforms an array into a sequence of cumulative totals. For an array nums of length n, the prefix sum array prefix has length n where prefix[i] = nums[0] + nums[1] + ... + nums[i]. This can be computed iteratively: Start with prefix[0] = nums[0], then for i from 1 to n-1, prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + nums[i]. Alternatively, we can compute a running sum without storing the entire array, which is what we do in higher levels. The intuition is that prefix sums allow us to quickly compute the sum of any subarray: sum from i to j = prefix[j] - prefix[i-1] (with prefix[-1] defined as 0). This is powerful because it reduces subarray sum queries from O(n) to O(1) after O(n) preprocessing. Time complexity to compute prefix sums is O(n). Space complexity is O(n) if we store the array, or O(1) extra if we just maintain a running sum. This feeds into L2 by providing the prefix sums needed for the difference transformation. Common mistakes include index errors when i=0 (need to handle prefix[i-1] as 0), or forgetting that prefix sums can be large or negative. Real code pattern: prefix = [0]*(n+1); for i in range(n): prefix[i+1] = prefix[i] + nums[i]. This uses an extra cell to simplify subarray sum: sum(i,j) = prefix[j+1] - prefix[i].

examples.trace:
Start: prefix[0] = nums[0] = 1
Step1: i=1, prefix[1] = prefix[0] + nums[1] = 1+2=3
Step2: i=2, prefix[2] = prefix[1] + nums[2] = 3+3=6
Step3: i=3, prefix[3] = prefix[2] + nums[3] = 6+4=10

Start: prefix[0] = 2
Step1: i=1, prefix[1] = 2 + (-1) = 1
Step2: i=2, prefix[2] = 1 + 3 = 4

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Sum Elements in a Contiguous Subarray
ladders.desc: Given start and end indices, compute the total of all elements between them inclusive.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental array operation: summing a contiguous block of elements. For an array nums, the sum from index i to j (inclusive) is nums[i] + nums[i+1] + ... + nums[j]. The naive approach iterates from i to j, adding each element, taking O(length) time where length = j-i+1. This is straightforward but inefficient if done repeatedly. The intuition is that we're aggregating values over a range. In pseudocode: sum = 0; for index from i to j: sum += nums[index]. Time complexity O(n) in worst case if the subarray spans the entire array. Space complexity O(1). This feeds into L3 because prefix sums optimize this operation to O(1) after preprocessing. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., including/excluding endpoints), or assuming indices are valid (i <= j and within bounds). Also, for empty subarrays (i > j), the sum is typically 0. This operation is the core of the original problem: we need to count how many such sums equal k.

examples.trace:
Subarray indices 1 to 2: nums[1]=2, nums[2]=3
Sum = 2 + 3 = 5

Subarray indices 0 to 3: nums[0]=5, nums[1]=-1, nums[2]=3, nums[3]=2
Sum = 5 + (-1) + 3 + 2 = 9

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Iterate Through an Array and Maintain a Running Total
ladders.desc: Traverse an array from start to end, accumulating a sum of elements seen so far.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: looping through an array and keeping a cumulative sum. Start with a variable (e.g., total = 0). For each element in the array, add its value to total. This gives the running total after processing each element. For example, for array [1,2,3], after first element total=1, after second total=3, after third total=6. The intuition is sequential aggregation—we're building up a result step by step. Pseudocode: total = 0; for num in array: total += num. Time complexity O(n) for n elements. Space complexity O(1). This feeds into L4 because summing a subarray is a specific case of this where we only add elements within a range. It also directly leads to L3's prefix sums, which are essentially running totals stored at each step. Common mistakes include not initializing total to 0, or modifying the array while iterating. For beginners, understand that arrays are zero-indexed, and loops can use indices or direct elements. This foundation is critical for almost all array processing algorithms.

examples.trace:
Start: total=0
Step1: num=2, total=0+2=2
Step2: num=3, total=2+3=5
Step3: num=4, total=5+4=9

Start: total=0
Step1: num=1, total=0+1=1
Step2: num=-1, total=1+(-1)=0
Step3: num=2, total=0+2=2
